question: A wire of length $L$ is hanging from a fixed support. The length changes from $L_{1}$ to $L_{2}$ when masses $M_{1}$ and $M_{2}$ are suspended respectively from its free end. Then $L$ is equal to
explanation:
L be original length of the wire. are tensions in the wire when masses are hung. When a mass is suspended from the wire, change in length of wire is $\Delta {L_1} = {L_1} - L$
When a mass is suspended from it, change in length of wire is $\Delta {L_2} = {L_2} - L$
From figure (2), ${T_1} = {M_1}g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
From figure (3), ${T_2} = {M_2}g\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
As Young’s modulus, $Y = \frac{{{T_1}L}}{{A\Delta {L_1}}} = \frac{{{T_2}L}}{{A\Delta {L_2}}}$
$\frac{{{M_1}g}}{{{L_1} - L}} = \frac{{{M_2}g}}{{{L_2} - L}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {{\text{Using}}\,{\text{(1)}}\,{\text{and}}\,{\text{(2)}}} \right]$$L = \frac{{{L_1}{M_2} - {L_2}{M_1}}}{{{M_2} - {M_1}}}.$

question: A steel rod of length $3\,m$ and cross-sectional area $1{m^2}$ ia acted upon by forces shown in figure.Determine the elongation of the length BC of the bar. Take $Y = 2.0 \times {10^{11}}\,N/{m^2}.$
explanation:
The bar is at equilibrium. The net force from right or left of a section of BC is $70\,KN$. We know that the extension due to external forces F is given
$\Delta l = \frac{{Fl}}{{AY}};\quad \Delta {l_{BC}} = \frac{{\left( {70 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times 1}}{{1 \times 2 \times {{10}^{11}}}} = 3.5 \times {10^{ - 7}}m$

question: One end of horizontal thick copper wire of length $2\,L$ and radius $2R$ is welded to an end of another horizontal thin copper wire of length $L$ and radus $R.$ When the arrangement is stretched by applying forces at two ends, the ratio of the elongation in the thin wire to that in the thick wire is
subject: Physics

question: A solid sphere of radius $r$ made of a soft material of bulk modulus $K$ is surrounded by a liquid in a cylindrical container. A massless piston of area a floats on the sueface of the liquid, convering entire cross section of cylindrical container. When a mass $m$ is placed on the surface of the piston to compress the liquid, the dfractional decrement in the radius of the sphere, $\left(\dfrac{d r}{r}\right)$, is:
explanation:
$\left[ {{\text{Bulk}}\,{\text{modules}} = \frac{{volumetric\,stress}}{{volumetric\,strain}}} \right]$
$ = \frac{{mg}}{{a\left( {\frac{{dV}}{V}} \right)}}$
$\frac{{dV}}{V} = \frac{{mg}}{{Ka}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Volume of sphere $\rightarrow V=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}$
Fractional change in volume
$\frac{{dV}}{V} = \frac{{3dr}}{r}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Using eq. (1) & (2) $\dfrac{3 d r}{r}=\dfrac{m g}{K a}$
$\dfrac{d r}{r}=\dfrac{m g}{3 K a}$
subject: Physics

question: Estimate the pressure deep inside the sea at a depth $\mathrm{h}$ below the surface. Assume that the density of water is $\rho_{0}$ at sea level and its bulk modulus is B. $P_{0}$ is the atomsphere pressure at sea level $P$ is the pressure at depth ' $h$ '.
explanation:
In a static fluid the pressure variation is given by
$\frac{{dP}}{{dh}} = \rho g \Rightarrow dp = \rho gdh\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The bulk modulus is defined as
$B=-\dfrac{d P}{d V / V}$
where $\dfrac{d V}{V}=\dfrac{-d \rho}{\rho}$
$dp = \frac{{ - BdV}}{V} = \frac{{Bd\rho }}{\rho }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Combining equations (1) and (2) we get
$\frac{{Bd\rho }}{\rho } = \rho gdh$
$\int_{{\rho _0}}^\rho {\frac{{d\rho }}{{{\rho ^2}}}} = \int_0^h {\frac{{gdh}}{B}} {\text{ or }}\frac{1}{{{\rho _0}}} - \frac{1}{\rho } = \frac{{gh}}{B}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
as $\quad d P=-\dfrac{B d V}{V}=B \dfrac{d \rho}{\rho}$
Hence $\int_{{p_0}}^p d P = \int_{{\rho _0}}^\rho B \frac{{d\rho }}{\rho }\$ or\$ P - {P_0} = B\ln \frac{\rho }{{{\rho _0}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
On multiplying equation (3) by $\rho_{0}$, we get
$1-\dfrac{\rho_{0}}{\rho}=\dfrac{\rho_{0} g h}{B}$
so that $\ln \dfrac{\rho}{\rho_{0}}=-\ln \left(1-\dfrac{\rho_{0} g h}{B}\right)$
Substituting this in equation (4), we get
$P=P_{0}-B \ln \left(1-\dfrac{\rho_{0} g h}{B}\right)$
subject: Physics

question: The density of water at the surface of the ocean is $\rho$. If the bulk modulus of water is B then, what is the density of ocean water at a depth, where the pressure is $n P_{0},\left(P_{0}\right.$ is the atmospheric pressure)
explanation:
Increase in pressure
$(\Delta P)=n P_{0}-P_{0}=(n-1) P_{0}$
The decrease in the volume due to increase in pressure $\Delta P$ is $\Delta V=\dfrac{V \Delta P}{B}$
$V^{\prime}=V-\Delta V=V-\dfrac{V \Delta P}{B}=\dfrac{V}{B}(B-\Delta P)$
$\rho^{\prime}=\dfrac{M}{V^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\rho V}{V^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\rho B}{B-\Delta P}=\dfrac{\rho B}{\left(B-(n-1) p_{0}\right)}$
subject: Physics

question: Consider a solid cylinder having length $\mathrm{L}$ and radius $a$. The upper end of the cylinder is fixed to the ceiling. The lower end of the cylinder is twisted by an angle $\theta$ then find the restoring torque produced in the rod. Given that $\eta$ is the shear modulus.
explanation:
Consider two points $P$ and $q$ as shown in the figure. After rotating the rod the point $q$ gets shifted to $q^{\prime}$. Let $\phi$ is the angle of shear.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955954/preptracker/questions/q_36250_explanation_1764955954190.png
From the figure $q q^{\prime}=a \theta=L \phi$
Consider a circular slice of radius $r$ and width dr as shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955955/preptracker/questions/q_36250_explanation_1764955955234.png
The small shear force on the slice is $\eta=\dfrac{d F / d A}{\tan \phi}$ $d F=d A \eta \tan \phi$
$\begin{aligned}& d F=(2 \pi r d r) \eta\left(\dfrac{r \theta}{L}\right) \\& d \tau=d F r=\dfrac{2 \pi \eta \theta r^{3} d r}{L} \\& \tau=\dfrac{2 \pi \eta \theta}{L} \int_{0}^{a} r^{3} d r \\& \tau=\dfrac{2 \pi \eta \theta}{L}\left[\dfrac{a^{4}}{4}\right]=\dfrac{\pi \eta \theta a^{4}}{2 L}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform rod of mass $m$, length $L$, area of crosssection A is rotated about an axis passing through one of its ends and perpendicular to its length with constant angular velocity $\omega$ in a horizontal plane. If Y is the Young's modulus of the material of rod, the increase in its length due to rotation of rod is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955953/preptracker/questions/q_36246_explanation_1764955952592.png
Consider a small element of length $d x$ at a distance $x$ from the axis of rotation.
Mass of the element, $d m=\dfrac{m}{L} d x=\mu d x$
The centripetal force acting on the element is $d T=d m \omega^{2} x=\mu \omega^{2} x d x$
The tension in the rod at a distance $x$ from the axis of rotation will be due to centripetal force due to all elements between $x=x$ to $x=L$
$\therefore T = \int_x^L \mu {\omega ^2}xdx = \frac{{\mu {\omega ^2}}}{2}\left[ {{L^2} - {x^2}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Let $d l$ be increses in length of the element. Then
$\begin{aligned}& Y=\dfrac{T / A}{d l / d x} \\& d l=\dfrac{T d x}{Y A}=\dfrac{\mu \omega^{2}}{2 Y A}\left[L^{2}-x^{2}\right] d x\end{aligned}$
[using (1)]
So the total elongation of the whole rod is
$\begin{aligned}& d l=\int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{\mu \omega^{2}}{2 Y A}\left[L^{2}-x^{2}\right] d x \\& \Delta l=\dfrac{\mu \omega^{2}}{2 Y A}\left[L^{2} x-\dfrac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{L} \\& =\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\mu \omega^{2} L^{3}}{Y A}=\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{m \omega^{2} L^{3}}{Y A}\end{aligned}$
Breaking Stress & Breaking Strain
subject: Physics

question: A metal wire of length $L_{1}$ and area of cross section A is attached to a rigid support. Another metal wire of length $L_{2}$ and of the same cross sectional area is attached to the free end of the first wire. A body of mass $M$ is then suspended from the free end of the second wire. If $Y_{1}$ and $Y_{2}$ are the Young's moduli of the wires respectively, the effective force constant of the system of two wires is
explanation:
The force constant of a wire is $K=\dfrac{Y A}{l}$ for series combination
$k_{e}=\dfrac{k_{1} k_{2}}{k_{1}+k_{2}}=\dfrac{Y_{1} Y_{2} A}{Y_{1} L_{2}+Y_{2} L_{1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A slightly conical wire of length $l$ and radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ is stretched by two forces each of magnitude $F$ applied parallel to length in opposite directions and normal to end faces. If $Y$ denotes the Young's modulus, then find the elongation of the wire $\left(r_{1}>r_{2}\right)$.
explanation:
Consider an element of length $\mathrm{d} x$ at distance $x$ as shown in the figure. The extension produced in the element $\mathrm{d} x$ is $\mathrm{d} l$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951659/preptracker/questions/q_36230_explanation_1764951658784.png
$\tan \theta=\dfrac{r_{2}-r_{1}}{l}=\dfrac{r-r_{1}}{x}$
$r-r_{1}=x \tan \theta$
$d r=d x \tan \theta$
$d l=\dfrac{F(d x)}{A Y}=\dfrac{F d x}{\pi r^{2} Y}$
Elongation of total wire is
$\begin{aligned}\Delta l & =\dfrac{F}{\pi Y} \int \dfrac{d r}{r^{2} \tan \theta} \\& =\dfrac{F}{\tan \theta \pi Y} \int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \dfrac{d r}{r^{2}} \\& =\dfrac{F l}{\pi Y\left(r_{2}-r_{1}\right)}\left[\dfrac{1}{r_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{r_{2}}\right] \\& =\dfrac{F l}{\pi r_{1} r_{2} Y}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A light rod of length $2\,m$ is suspended from the ceiling horizontally by means of two vertical wires of equal length tied to its end. One of the wires is made of steel of cross-section $0.1\,c{m^2}$. The other wire is of brass of cross-section $0.2\,c{m^2}$. A weight is suspended from a certain point of the rod such that equal stresses are produced in both the wires. The rod remians horizontal in this case also. Find out the position of the load from the steel wire.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955946/preptracker/questions/q_36226_explanation_1764955946182.png
strees $=\dfrac{T}{A} \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{1}}{A_{1}}=\dfrac{T_{2}}{A_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
and for equilibrium,
$m g=T_{1}+T_{2}=\dfrac{3 T_{2}}{2}$
and $\quad m g x-T_{2} \cdot(2)=0$
$\dfrac{3 T_{2}}{2} x-2 T_{2}=0 \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{4}{3} m$

question: For homogenous isotropic material, which one of the following cannot be the value of Poisson's ratio?
explanation:
The Poisson's ratio for homogenous isotropic material must be greater than -1 and less than 0.5 for the elastic moduli to have finite positive values.
Hence, 0.8 cannot be value of Poisson's ratio for homogenous isotropic material

question: The bulk modulus of water is $2.1 \times {10^9}\,N/{m^2}$. The pressure required to increase the density of water by $0.1 \%$ is
explanation:

$\frac{{0.1}}{{100}} = \frac{{dp}}{{2.1 \times {{10}^9}}}$
$\therefore dp = 2.1 \times {10^6}\,N/{m^2}$

question: A uniform cube is subjected to volume compression. If each side is decreased by $1 \%$, then bulk strain is
explanation:
If side of the cube is$L\,{\text{then}}\,V = {L^3}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{V} = 3\frac{{dL}}{L}$

$=3 \times 1 \%=3 \%$
Therefore, Bulk strain $\frac{{\Delta V}}{V} = 0.03$

question: The compressibility of water is $5 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,{m^2}/N.$ Pressure of  $15 \times {10^6}\,Pa$ is applied on $100\,mL$ volume of water. The change in the volume of water is
explanation:
The compressibility of water is given by
$k = - \frac{{\Delta V}}{{\Delta pV}}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta V = - k\Delta pV$
$ = - \left( {5 \times {{10}^{ - 10}} \times 15 \times {{10}^6} \times 100} \right)ml$
$ = - 0.75ml$
Thus, volume of the water decreases by $0.75ml.$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of hydraulic stress to the corresponding strain is known as
explanation: The ratio of hydraulic stress to the corresponding strain is known as bulk modulus.

question: The young's modulus of brass and steel are respectively $1.0 \times {10^{10}}\,N/{m^2}$ and $2 \times {10^{10}}\,N/{m^2}$. A brass wire and a steel wire of the same length are extended by $1\,mm$ under the same force, the radii of brass and steel wires are $R_{B}$ and $R_{S}$ respectively. Then
explanation:

question: Longitudinal stress of  $1\,kg/m{m^2}$ is applied on a wire. The percentage increase in length is $\left( {y = {{10}^{11}}\,N/{m^2}} \right)$
explanation:
Longitudinal strain $=\dfrac{\text { stress }}{\mathrm{Y}}=\dfrac{10^{6}}{10^{11}}=10^{-5}$
Percentage increase in length $=$ $10^{-5} \times 100=0.001 \%$

question: The following four wires are made of same material. Which of these will have the largest extension when the same tension is applied?
explanation:
As $\mathrm{Y}=\dfrac{\mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{L}}{\Delta \mathrm{L} \times \mathrm{A}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{mg} \cdot \mathrm{L}}{\Delta \mathrm{L} \cdot \mathrm{A}}$ or $\Delta \mathrm{L}=\dfrac{\mathrm{mgL}}{\mathrm{YA}}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{L} \propto \dfrac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A}}$, which is maximum for option (2).
subject: Physics

question: A wire of length ' $L$ ' and area of cross section ' $A$ ' is made of material of Young's modulus ' $Y$ '. It is stretched by an amount ' $x$ '. The work done in stretching the wire is
explanation:
Force F required to stretch the wire by an amount l is given by
$F = \frac{{YA}}{L}l$
$\therefore $ The work done in stretching the wire for a total extension of $x$ is,
$W = \int_0^x {F.dl = \int_0^x {\frac{{YA}}{L}ldl = \frac{{YA}}{L}\left[ {\frac{{{l^2}}}{2}} \right]_0^x} } $
$ \Rightarrow W = \frac{{YA{x^2}}}{{2L}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two rods of same material and volume having circular cross - section are subjected to tension $T$. Within the elastic limit, same force is applied to both the rods. Diameter of the first rod is half of the second rod, then the extensions of first rod to second rod will be in the ratio
explanation:
As rods are made of same material, their Young's moduli are same.
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\dfrac{F_{1}}{A_{1}}}{\dfrac{\Delta L_{1}}{L_{1}}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{F_{2}}{A_{2}}}{\dfrac{\Delta L_{2}}{L_{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F_{1} L_{1}}{\Delta L_{1} \times 2 \pi\left(\dfrac{d_{1}}{2}\right) L_{1}}=\dfrac{F_{2} L_{2}}{\Delta L_{2} \times 2 \pi\left(\dfrac{d_{2}}{2}\right) L_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{F_{1}}{\Delta L_{1} \times d_{1}}=\dfrac{F_{2}}{\Delta L_{2} \times d_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta L_{1}}{\Delta L_{2}}=\dfrac{F_{1}}{F_{2}} \times \dfrac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}$
Here, $F_{1}=F_{2}$ and $d_{1}=\dfrac{d_{2}}{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta L_{1}}{\Delta L_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{1}$
As $r_{A}=2 r_{B} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\sigma_{B}}{\sigma_{\mathrm{A}}}=2^{2}=4$
subject: Physics

question: Two wires $A$ and $B$ are stretched by the same load. If the area of cross-section of wire $A$ is double that of $B$, then the stress on $B$ is
explanation:
As both wires A and B are stretched by the same load ( W)
${\text{stres}}{{\text{s}}_A} = \frac{W}{{{A_A}}}$
$(\text { stress })_{B}=\dfrac{W}{A_{B}}$
where $A_{A}$ and $A_{B}$ are the areas of cross-section of wires $A$ and $B$ respectively.
Thus, $\dfrac{(\text { stress })_{B}}{(\text { stress })_{A}}=\dfrac{A_{A}}{A_{B}}$
Given that $A_{A}=2 A_{B}$
$\therefore \dfrac{(\text { stress })_{\mathrm{B}}}{(\text { stress })_{\mathrm{A}}}=2$
subject: Physics

question: For steel  $Y = 2 \times {10^{11}}\,N/{m^2}$. The force required to double the length of a steel wire of area $1\,c{m^2}$ is
explanation:
Stress required to double the length is called Young's modulus
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \mathrm{Y}=\dfrac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{A}} \text { or } \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{Y} . \mathrm{A} \\& \mathrm{A}=2 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-4}=2 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Copper of fixed volume $\mathrm{V}$ is drawn into wire of length $l$. When this wire is subjected to a constant force $F$, the extension produced in the wire is $\Delta l$. Which of the following graphs is a straight line?
explanation:
Young's modulus is given by
${\text{Y}} = \frac{{{\text{F}} \times l}}{{\,{\text{A}} \times \Delta l}}\quad (\because {\text{Hooke'sLaw}})\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As $V = A \times l = {\text{constant}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{(2)}}$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$Y = \frac{{F \times {l^2}}}{{\,V \times \Delta l}}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta l = \frac{F}{{V \times Y}} \times {l^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta l \propto {l^2}$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical wires of substances ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' and ' $\mathrm{Q}$ ' are subjected to equal stretching force along the length. If the elongation of ' $Q$ ' is more than that of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ', then
explanation: Elasticity is the ability of a body to resist an external force causing deformation/ distortion. Given ' $Q$ ' is more elongated than $P$ when subjected to same stretching force, $P$ has more resisting strength than $Q$, i.e., $P$ is more elastic than $Q$.

explanation: Greater the value of Young's modulus of a substance, higher is the elasticity. Out of the given substances, steel has greater value of Young's modulus.

explanation: $\mathrm{Y}=\dfrac{\mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\dfrac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}}=\dfrac{\text { Tensile stress }}{\text { Longitudinal strain }}$

question: Two wires of different materials have same length $\mathrm{L}$ and same diameter $\mathrm{d}$. The second wire is connected at the end of the first wire and forms one single wire of double the length. This wire is subjected to streching force $\mathrm{F}$ to produce the elongation $l$. The two wires have
explanation:
Stress $=\dfrac{\text { force }}{\text { area }}$.
As force on both wire is same and their areas are also same, stresses on both wires are equal. Strain on the wire is given by
Strain $=\dfrac{\text { Stress }}{Y}$
where $Y$ is Young's modulus.
For different materials the values of Young's moduli are different
Hence, strain for both wires are different.

question: What is the approximate change in density of water in a lake at a depth of $400\,m$ below the surface? The density of water at the surface is $1030\,kg/{m^2}$ and bulk modulus of water is $2 \times {10^9}\,N/{m^2}$

question: The density of a metal at normal pressure is $\rho$. When it is subjected to an excess pressure $p$, the density becomes $\rho^{\prime}$. If $K$ is the bulk modulus of the metal, then the ratio $\dfrac{\rho^{\prime}}{\rho}$ is
explanation:
The bulk modulus of metal is
$\mathrm{K}=-\dfrac{\Delta p}{\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}} \Rightarrow \Delta V=-\dfrac{p V}{K}$
$[\because$ excess pressure $\Delta p=p]$
New volume of metal
$V^{\prime}=V+\Delta V=V-\dfrac{p V}{K}=V\left(1-\dfrac{p}{K}\right)$
Since, mass of metal remains same,
$\begin{aligned}& \rho \mathrm{V}=\rho^{\prime} \mathrm{V}^{\prime} \\\Rightarrow & \dfrac{\rho^{\prime}}{\rho}=\dfrac{V}{V^{\prime}}=\dfrac{V}{V\left(1-\dfrac{p}{K}\right)}=\dfrac{1}{\left(1-\dfrac{p}{K}\right)}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A toy cart is tied to the end of an unstretched string of length $l$. When revolved, the toy cart moves in horizontal circle with radius $2 l$ and time period $T$. If it is speeded until it moves in horizontal circle of radius $3 l$ with period $T_{1}$, relation between $T$ and $T_{1}$ is (Hooke's law is obeyed)
explanation:
When the length of the strected string is $2 l$ the tension is,
$F=\sigma A=\left(Y \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}\right) A=Y \dfrac{(2 l-l)}{l} A=Y A,$
where A is the cross-sectional area of the string and $Y$ is the Young's Modulus of the string material. Similarly when the length of the stretched string is $3 l$ the tension is,
$F_{1}=Y \dfrac{(3 l-l)}{l} A=2 Y A$
For uniform circular motion, $F = mr{\omega ^2}$, where $r$ and $\omega $ are the radius of the circular path and angular velocity respectively of the toy cart of mass $m$.
$\therefore \frac{F}{{{F_1}}} = \frac{{m(2l){\omega ^2}}}{{\,m(3l)\omega _1^2}} \Rightarrow \frac{{YA}}{{2YA}} = \frac{{2{\omega ^2}}}{{3\omega _1^2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\omega^{2}}{\omega_{1}^{2}}=\dfrac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{T_{1}}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{1}^{2}}{T^{2}}=\dfrac{3}{4}$
$\Rightarrow T_{1}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} T$
subject: Physics

question: Two metal wires $P$ and  $Q$ of same length and material are stretched by same load. Their masses are in the ratio $m_{1}: m_{2}$. The ratio of elongations of wire $P$ to that of $Q$  is
explanation:
Young's modulus (Y) $=\dfrac{F l}{A \Delta l} \Rightarrow \Delta l=\dfrac{F l}{A Y}$
Where $A = $ area of cross-section of wire
$\Delta l=$ change in the length of the wire $l=$ length of wire and
$F = $ applied force
Since F, $l$ and $Y$ are same for both wires,
$\Delta l \propto \frac{1}{A}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
but $m=\rho \mathrm{V}$
where, $\rho=$ density, $V=$ volume
$\Rightarrow m=\rho A l[\because V=A l]$
$ \Rightarrow m \propto A\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq.(1) and eq.(2), we get
$\dfrac{\Delta l_{1}}{\Delta l_{2}}=\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}=\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A wire of length $L$ and radius $r$ is rigidly fixed at one end. On stretching the other end of the wire with a force $F$, the increase in length is $l$. If another wire of the same material but double the length and radius is stretched with a force $2\,F$, then increase in length is
explanation:
As the wire of length $L$ and radius $r$ when stretched with a force $\mathrm{F}$ elongates by $l$, Young's modulus
$Y = \frac{\sigma }{\smallint } = \frac{{\left( {\frac{F}{A}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{l}{{\,L}}} \right)}} = \frac{{FL}}{{Al}} = \frac{{FL}}{{\pi {r^2}l}}$
When the length, radius and force are doubled, elongation becomes,
$l^{\prime}=\in^{\prime} \mathrm{L}^{\prime}=\dfrac{\sigma^{\prime}}{\mathrm{Y}}(2 \mathrm{~L})=\dfrac{\left[\dfrac{2 \mathrm{~F}}{\pi(2 \mathrm{r})^{2}}\right]}{\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{FL}}{\pi r^{2} l}\right)}(2 \mathrm{~L})=l$
subject: Physics

question: The following four wires of length $L$ and radius $r$ are made of the same material. Which of these will have the largest extension, when the same tension is applied?
explanation:
Young's modulus, $Y=\dfrac{F}{A} \dfrac{L}{\Delta L}=\dfrac{T}{\pi r^{2}} \dfrac{L}{\Delta L}$
$\Delta L=\dfrac{T}{\pi r^{2}} \dfrac{L}{Y}$
where the symbols have their usual meaning. As the four wires are made of the same material, therefore Young's modulus is the same four wires. As the $T\,{\text{and}}\,Y$ are the same for the four wires.
$\therefore \Delta L \alpha \dfrac{L}{r^{2}}$
$\dfrac{L}{r^{2}}$ is maximum for wire of length $L = 100\,cm$ and radius $r = 0.2\,mm$

question: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. The first wire has crosssectional area A and the second wire has crosssectional area $3A$. If the length of the first wire is increased by $l$ on applying a force $F$, how much force is needed to stretch the second wire by the same amount ?
explanation:

$L_{2}=\dfrac{L_{1}}{3}$
$\text { Stress }=Y(\text { strain })$
$F=\left(\dfrac{A Y}{L}\right) \Delta L$

question:

As, restoring force is zero for a plastic body.
$\therefore \quad Y=0$
Therefore, Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion. Option (1) is correct.

Since, compressibility is defined as the dfractional change in volume per unit increase or decrease in pressure.
$\begin{gathered}K=1 / B \\B=\left(\dfrac{-\Delta P}{\Delta V / V}\right)\end{gathered}$
Where, $B$ is bulk modulus and $\Delta p$ change in pressure. So, in gases, dfractional change in volume with per unit increase or decrease in pressure is not very prominent. Thus, they have large compressibility.

question:
Assertion: When a solid sphere is placed in the fluid under high pressure, then it is compressed uniformly on all sides.


Reason: The force applied by fluids acts in perpendicular direction at each point of surface.

question: Determine the volume contraction of a solid copper cube, $10\,cm$ on an edge, when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of  $7 \times {10^6}\,Pa$. $K$ for copper $ = 140 \times {10^6}\,Pa$.
explanation:
Given, each side of cube
$(l) = 10\,cm = 0.1\,m$
Hydraulic pressure $(p) = 7 \times {10^6}\,Pa$
Bulk modulus for copper $(k) = 140 \times {10^9}\,Pa$
Volume of the cube $(V) = {l^3} = {(0.1)^3} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}$
Bulk modulus for copper $(K)=\dfrac{p}{\Delta V / V}=\dfrac{p V}{\Delta V}$
$\Delta V = \frac{{pV}}{K} = \frac{{7 \times {{10}^6} \times 1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{140 \times {{10}^9}}} = 5 \times {10^{ - 8}}\,{m^3}$

question: A rigid bar of mass $15\,kg$ is supported symmetrically by three wires each $2\,m$ long. These at each end are of copper and middle one is of iron. Determine the ratio of their diameters if each is to have the same tension. Young's modulus of elasticity for copper and steel are $110 \times {10^9}\,N/{m^2}$ and  $190 \times {10^9}\,N/{m^2}$ respectively.
explanation:
Young's modulus of copper
$\begin{aligned}& (Y)=\dfrac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }}=\dfrac{F / A}{\text { strain }} \\\therefore \quad & Y \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}} \text { or } d^{2} \propto \dfrac{1}{Y}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \frac{{d_1^2}}{{d_2^2}} = \frac{{{Y_2}}}{{{Y_1}}}$
$\frac{{{d_1}}}{{{d_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{Y_2}}}{{{Y_1}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{190 \times {{10}^9}}}{{110 \times {{10}^9}}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{19}}{{11}}} = \sqrt {1.73} = 1.31$
$\therefore \quad {d_1}:{d_2} = 1.31:1$
subject: Physics

question: A steel wire of length $4.7\,m$ and cross-sectional area $3.0 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{m^2}$ stretches by the same amount as a copper wire of length $3.5\,m$ and crosssectional area $4.0 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{m^2}$ under given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of steel to that of copper is
explanation:
Given, for steel wire
${\text{ Length }}\left( {{l_1}} \right) = 4.7\,m$
${\text{Area}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{cross - section}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{steel}}\,{\text{cable}}\left( {{A_1}} \right) = 3.0 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{m^2}$
For copper wire
${\text{ Length }}\left( {{l_2}} \right) = 3.5\,m$
Area of cross-section $\left( {{A_2}} \right) = 4.0 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{m^2}$
Let $F$ be the given load under which steel and copper wires be stretched by the same amount $\Delta l$.
Young's modulus $(Y)=\dfrac{F / A}{\Delta l / l}=\dfrac{F \times l}{A \times \Delta l}$
For steel, $\quad {Y_s} = \frac{{F \times {l_1}}}{{{A_1} \times \Delta l}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For copper, $\quad {Y_c} = \frac{{F \times {l_2}}}{{{A_2} \times \Delta l}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{Y_{s}}{Y_{c}}=\dfrac{F \times l_{1}}{A_{1} \times \Delta l} \times \dfrac{A_{2} \times \Delta l}{F \times l_{2}} \\= & \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}} \times \dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}=\dfrac{4.7}{3.5} \times \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}}=1.8\end{aligned}$

$\qquad \left( {\because 1\,atm = 1.013 \times {{10}^5}pa} \right)$
$ = 1.013 \times {10^6}pa$
Bulk modulus for glass $(K) = 37 \times {10^9}\,N/{m^2}$
Fractional change in volume $\left(\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right)=$ ?
Bulk modulus $(K)=\dfrac{P}{\Delta V / V}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=\dfrac{P}{K}=\dfrac{1.013 \times 10^{6}}{37 \times 10^{9}}=2.74 \times 10^{-5}$
$\therefore$ Fractional change in volume
$\left(\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right)=2.74 \times 10^{-5}$

question: A constant force $F_{0}$ is applied on a uniform elastic rod placed over a smooth horizontal surface as shown in figure. Young's modulus of rod is $Y$ and area of cross section is $S$. The strain produced in the rod in the direction of force is
explanation:
Acceleartion $=\dfrac{F_{0}}{\lambda L}$, where $\lambda=$ mass per unit length, tension at a distance $x$ from front end
$T=\lambda(L-x) a=\dfrac{\lambda(L-x) F_{0}}{\lambda L}=F_{0}\left(1-\dfrac{x}{L}\right)$
Consider a small element of length $\mathrm{d} x$ at a distance $x$ from the front end.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951589/preptracker/questions/q_35872_explanation_1764951589415.png
The change in the length of the element is $d l$.
$d l=\dfrac{T d x}{S Y}$
$\therefore$ Total change in length $=\int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{F_{0}}{S Y}\left(1-\dfrac{x}{L}\right) \cdot d x$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta l=\dfrac{F_{0}}{S Y}\left|x-\dfrac{x^{2}}{2 L}\right|_{0}^{L}=\dfrac{F_{0}}{S Y}\left(L-\dfrac{L}{2}\right)=\dfrac{F_{0} L}{2 S Y} \\& \therefore \text { Total strain }=\dfrac{F_{0}}{2 S Y}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A wire of length $2 L$ and radius $r$ is stretched between $A$ and $B$. If $Y$ is the Young's modulus of the wire and it is stretched like $ACB$, then the tension in the wire will be $(d <  < 2 L)$
explanation:
$T=\dfrac{Y A \Delta L}{2 L}$
Increase in total length of the wire is
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta L=2 \sqrt{L^{2}+d^{2}}-2 L \\& \Delta L=2 L\left[1+\dfrac{d^{2}}{L^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2}-2 L \\& \Delta L=2 L\left[1+\dfrac{d^{2}}{2 L^{2}}\right]-2 L=\dfrac{d^{2}}{L} \\& T=\dfrac{Y \pi r^{2} d^{2}}{2 L^{2}} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The length of an elastic string is $a$ metre when the longitudinal tension is $4\,N$ and $b$ metre when the longitudinal tension is $5\,N$. the length of the string in meter when the longitudinal tension is $9\,N$ is
explanation:
Let $L$ is the original length of the wire and $\mathrm{K}$ is force constant of wire.
Final length $=$ initial length + elongation
$L^{\prime}=L+\dfrac{F}{K}$
For first condition $a = L + \frac{4}{K}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For second condition $b = L + \frac{5}{K}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
By solving (1) and (2) equations we get
$L=5 a-4 b \text { and } K=\dfrac{1}{b-a}$
Now when the longitudinal tension is $9\,N$, length of the string
$=L+\dfrac{9}{K}=5 a-4 b+9(b-a)=5 b-4 a .$
subject: Physics

question: The mean density of sea water is $\rho$, and bulk modulus is $B$. The change in density of sea water in going from the surface of water to a depth of $h$ is

Bulk modulus
$\begin{gathered}B=\dfrac{-\Delta P}{\Delta V / V} ; \quad V=\dfrac{m}{\rho} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d V}{V}=\dfrac{-d \rho}{\rho} \\\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=\dfrac{-\Delta \rho}{\rho} \\\therefore \Delta \rho=\dfrac{\rho \Delta P}{B}=\dfrac{\rho(\rho g h)}{B}=\dfrac{\rho^{2} g h}{B}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: The average depth of indian Ocean is about $3000\,m$. The dfractional compression, $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}$ of water at the bottom of the ocean is (Given: Bulk modulus of the wear $ = 2.2 \times {10^9}\,N/{m^{ - 2}}$ and  $\left. {g = 10m{s^{ - 2}}} \right)$
explanation:

$P = h\rho g = 3000\,m \times 100kg{m^{ - 3}} \times 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
Fractional compression $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}$ is
$\frac{{\Delta V}}{V} = \frac{P}{B} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^7}N{m^{ - 2}}}}{{2.2 \times {{10}^9}N{m^{ - 2}}}} = 1.36 \times {10^{ - 2}} = 1.36\% $

question: A uniform elastic rod of cross-section area $A$, natural length $L$ and Young's modulus $Y$ is placed on a smooth horizontal surface. Now two horizontal forces (of magnitude $F\,{\text{and}}\,3\,F$ ) directed along the length of rod and in opposite direction act at two of its ends as shown. After the rod has acquired steady state, the extension of the rod will be
explanation:
The acceleration of the rod is $\dfrac{2 F}{m}$, where $\mathrm{m}$ is the mass of the rod. The tension present in the rod at a distance $x$ from the left end is T.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951577/preptracker/questions/q_35809_explanation_1764951576945.png
$\begin{aligned}& T-F=\mu x a=\mu x\left(\dfrac{2 F}{m}\right) \\& T=F+\dfrac{2 \mu x F}{m}\left(\mu=\dfrac{m}{L}\right) \\& T=F+\dfrac{2 F x}{L}\end{aligned}$
Consider a small element of length $d x$. The extension produced in the element is $d l$.
$d l=\dfrac{F d x}{A Y}=\dfrac{1}{A Y}\left(F+\dfrac{2 F x}{L}\right) d x$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta l=\dfrac{F}{A Y} \int_{0}^{L}\left(1+\dfrac{2 x}{L}\right) d x \\& =\dfrac{F}{A Y}\left[[x]_{0}^{L}+\dfrac{2}{L}\left[\dfrac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{L}\right] \\& =\dfrac{F}{A Y}\left[L+\dfrac{2}{L} \dfrac{L^{2}}{2}\right]=\dfrac{2 F L}{A Y}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A steel ring of radius $r$ and cross-section area ' $A$ ' is fitted onto a wooden disc of radius $R(R > r)$. IF Young's modulus be E, then the force with which the steel ring is expanded is
explanation:
Initial length (circumference) of the ring $=2 \pi r$
Final length (circumference) of the ring $=2 \pi R$
Change in length $=2 \pi R-2 \pi r$.
strain $=\dfrac{\text { change in length }}{\text { original length }}=\dfrac{2 \pi(R-r)}{2 \pi r}=\dfrac{R-r}{r}$
Now Young's modulus $E=\dfrac{F / A}{\Delta l / l}=\dfrac{F / A}{(R-r) / r}$
$\therefore F=A E\left(\dfrac{R-r}{r}\right)$.
subject: Physics

question: A student performs an experiment to determine the Young's modulus of a wire, exactly $2 m$ long, by searle's method. The student measures the extension in the length of the wire to be $0.8\,mm$ with an uncertainty of $ \pm 0.05\,mm$ at a load of exactly $1.0\,kg$. The student also,measures the diameter of the wire to be $0.4\,mm$ with an uncertainty $ \pm 0.01\,mm.\,{\text{Take}}\,g = 9.8\,m/{s^2}$ (exact). The Young's modulus obtained from the reading is
explanation:
$Y=\dfrac{F L}{A l}=\dfrac{4 F L}{\pi d^{2} l} ; F=m g$
Substituting the values, we get $Y = 2 \times {10^{11}}\,N/{m^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta Y}}{Y} = 2\frac{{\Delta d}}{d} + \frac{{\Delta l}}{l} = 2\left( {\frac{{0.01}}{{0.4}}} \right) + \frac{{0.05}}{{0.8}} = \frac{9}{{80}}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta Y = \frac{9}{{80}} \times Y = \frac{9}{{80}} \times 2 \times {10^{11}} = 0.2 \times {10^{11}}\,N/{m^2}.{\text{ }}$
subject: Physics

question:
Arubber cord $10\,m$ long is suspended vertically. How much does it stretch under its own weight (Density of rubber is

explanation:
A rod of length $L$ and density $\rho$, is fixed to rigid support. Consider a small element of length $d x$ present at a distance $x$ from the lower end of the rod. Let $\rho$ is the linear density of the rod.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955924/preptracker/questions/q_35794_explanation_1764955924082.png

${\text{Where}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,F = \rho Axg$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,dl = \frac{{\rho gdx}}{Y}$

question: A uniform cylindrical wire is subjected to a longitudinal tensile stress of $5 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. The Young's modulus of the material of the wire is $2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$. The volume change in the wire is $0.02 \%$. The fractional change in the radius is
explanation:
$Y=\dfrac{\text { stress }}{\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}=\dfrac{\text { stress }}{Y}=2.5 \times 10^{-4}$
Volume of a cylinder is $V=\pi r^{2} L$

question: There is no change in the volume of a wire due to the change in its length on stretching. The Poisson's ratio of the material of the wire is:
explanation:
Volume of cylindrical wire, $V=\dfrac{\pi x^{2} L}{4}$ where $x$ is diameter of wire
Differentiating both sides
$\dfrac{d V}{d x}=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\left[2 x L+x^{2} \cdot \dfrac{d L}{d x}\right]$
Also volume remains constant $\therefore \dfrac{d V}{d x}=0$
$\begin{gathered}\therefore \quad 2 x L+x^{2} \dfrac{d L}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow 2 x L=-x^{2} \dfrac{d L}{d x}, \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{\dfrac{d x}{x}}{\dfrac{d L}{L}}=-\dfrac{1}{2}\end{gathered}$
$\Rightarrow \text { Poisson's ratio }=-\dfrac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A rod has Poisson's ratio 0.2. If a rod suffers longitudinal strain of $2 \times 10^{-3}$, then the percentage change in volume is

question: The bulk modulus of water if its volume changes from 100 litre to 99.5 litre under a pressure of $100 \mathrm{~atm}$ is (Take  ; $1\,atm = {10^5}N{m^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
According to definition of bulk modulus,

question:

$\left[\because \varepsilon=\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}\right]$
$=8 \times 10^{-1}=0.8 {~J}$
So, the correct option is (2).

question: An object of mass $15\,kg$ is attached to the end of a metal wire of unstretched length $1.0\,m$. The object is then whirled in a vertical circle with an angular velocity of $4 {rad} / {s}$ at the bottom of the circle. If the cross sectional area of the wire is $0.05 {~cm}^{2}$ and Young's modulus of metal is $2 \times 10^{11} {~N} / {m}^{2}$, then the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of its path (take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
At lowest point during the motion in vertical circle,
$T-m g=m \omega^{2} l$
$T=m g+m \omega^{2} l=m\left(g+\omega^{2} l\right)$
$=15\left(10+4^{2} \times 1\right)=390 {~N}$
We know that, Young's modulus,
$Y=\dfrac{\text { Stress }}{\text { Strain }}=\dfrac{T / A}{\Delta l / l} \Rightarrow Y=\dfrac{T l}{A \Delta l}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \Delta l=\dfrac{T l}{A Y}=\dfrac{390 \times 1}{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{11}} {~m}$
$=390 \times 10^{-6} m=0.39 \times 10^{-3} m=0.39 m m$
So, the correct option is (2).

question: A uniform rod of length $L$ is rotated in a horizontal plane about a vertical axis through one of its ends. The angular speed of rotation is $\omega$. Find the increase in length of the rod, if $\rho$ and $Y$ are the density and Young's modulus of the rod respectively.
explanation:
When rod is rotated in a horizontal plane, centrifugal force is responsible for increasing the length of rod, which also produces the stress. If $d l$ be the change in length of length $x$ of the rod by application of force $d F$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958102/preptracker/questions/q_109606_explanation_1764958101843.png Young's modulus,
$Y=\dfrac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }}=\dfrac{d F / A}{d l / x}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad dl = \frac{{dF \cdot x}}{{AY}} \Rightarrow dF = \frac{{AYdl}}{x}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where, $d F=$ centrifugal force due to mass of the element of length, $d x$.
$d F=d m \cdot x \omega^{2}$
$dF = \frac{m}{L} \cdot dx\,x{\omega ^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\left[ {\because \,\,{\text{Change }}\,\,\,{\text{in }}\,\,\,{\text{mass}}\,\,\,dm = \frac{m}{L}dx} \right]$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{A Y d l}{x}=\dfrac{m}{L} \cdot d x\,x \omega^{2}$
$\Rightarrow d l=\dfrac{m x^{2} \omega^{2}}{L A Y} \cdot d x$
$\Rightarrow$ Total change in length,
$l=\int d l=\int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{m x^{2} \omega^{2}}{L A Y} d x$
$=\dfrac{m}{A L} \cdot \dfrac{\omega^{2} L^{3}}{3 Y}=\dfrac{\rho \omega^{2} L^{3}}{3 Y}[\because m=\rho A L]$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

explanation:
A stretched catapult has elastic potential energy stored in it(elastic potential energy stored in both rubber strings)

$U = \frac{1}{2}Fx = \frac{{{F^2}L}}{{2AY}}.\quad U \propto \frac{L}{{{r^2}}}$ [F and Y are constant]
$\therefore \quad \frac{{{U_A}}}{{{U_B}}} = \left( {\frac{{{L_A}}}{{{L_B}}}} \right) \times {\left( {\frac{{{r_B}}}{{{r_A}}}} \right)^2} = (3) \times {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{3}{4}.$

question: If in a wire of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $l$ and Young's modulus $Y$, longitudinal strain $x$ is produced then the potential energy stored in it will be
explanation:
Elastic potential energy per unit volume is
$u=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \operatorname{stress} \times \operatorname{strain}$
$\mathrm{Y}=\dfrac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }} \Rightarrow \text { stress }=\mathrm{Y} \times \text { strain }$
Given, strain $=x$
Therefore, $u=\dfrac{1}{2} Y x^{2} \Rightarrow u=0.5 Y x^{2}$
$U=u V=u A l=\dfrac{Y x^{2} A l}{2}$

question: A smooth uniform string of natural length ' $l$ ', cross-sectional area A and Young's modulus $Y$ is pulled along its length by a force $F$ on a horizontal surface. Find the elastic potential energy stored in the string.
explanation:
The tension $T$ in the string at a distance $x$ from its free end is given as:
$T=\dfrac{F}{l} x ;$
$\text { Stress }=\dfrac{T}{A}=\dfrac{F}{A l} x$
Potential energy $U=\int \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{(\text { stress })^{2}}{Y} d V$
$U=\dfrac{1}{2 Y} \int_{0}^{l} \dfrac{F^{2}}{A^{2} l^{2}} x^{2} d v$
where $d V=A d x$; This gives $U=\dfrac{F^{2} l}{6 A Y}$

question:
Read the ${\rm{Statement - A}}$ and ${\rm{Statement - B}}$ carefully to mark the correct option given below:
For small deformations in materials, stress is directly proportional to strain according to Hooke's Law.
Hooke's Law is applicable to all materials regardless of the amount of deformation they undergo.
explanation:
Statement A is true. As per Hooke's Law, for small deformations, the stress and strain are proportional to each other. This is a fundamental concept in material science which suggests that within the elastic limit, the amount of deformation (strain) is directly proportional to the applied load (stress).
Statement B is false. Hooke's Law is not applicable to all materials or under all conditions. It is an empirical law and is valid only for many materials that exhibit linear elastic behavior when subjected to small deformations. There are materials, such as elastomers, that do not follow Hooke's Law throughout their range of deformation. Additionally, beyond the proportional limit, the stress and strain are not directly proportional, and materials may not return to their original shape and size upon the removal of the applied force. Correct Option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question: The point of maximum and minimum attraction in the curve between potential energy $(U)$ and distance $(r)$ of a diatomic molecule are respectively.
explanation:
The relation between force and potential energy is $F=-\left(\dfrac{d U}{d x}\right)$
In the region between $T\,{\text{and}}\,R$
$\dfrac{d U}{d x}=(+) v e \Rightarrow F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x}=(-) v e$
In the region between $P{\mkern 1mu} {\text{and}}{\mkern 1mu} R$
$\dfrac{d U}{d x}=(-) v e \Rightarrow F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x}=(+) v e$
The region between $T$ and $R$ is attractive and between $P$ and $R$ is repulsive.
At-R
$R \dfrac{d U}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow F=0$ and the molecule is in equilibrium.
At-S
$\begin{aligned}& S \dfrac{d U}{d x}=(+) v e \\\Rightarrow & F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x}=-(v e)\end{aligned}$
The slope $\dfrac{d U}{d x}$ is maximum at $S$ and minimum at $T$. Attractive force is maximum at $\mathrm{S}$ and minimum at $T$.

question: A uniform ring of mass $M$ of outside radius $r_{2}$ is fitted tightly with a shaft of radius $r_{1}$. If the shaft is rotated with a constant angular acceleration $\alpha$ about it's axis, the moment of the elastic force in the ring about the axis of rotation is
explanation:

$\sigma=\dfrac{M}{\pi\left(r_{2}^{2}-r_{1}^{2}\right)}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956920/preptracker/questions/q_36234_explanation_1764956919642.png
The toque required to rotate the slice with angular acceleration $\alpha$ is
$\begin{aligned}& d \tau=\left(d m a_{t}\right) x=(d m \alpha x) x \\& d \tau=d m \alpha x^{2}=\mu(2 \pi x d x) \alpha x^{2} \\& \int d \tau=2 \pi \mu x \alpha \int x^{3} d x \\& \tau=\dfrac{2 \pi \alpha M}{\pi r_{2}^{2}-r_{1}^{2}} \int_{1_{1}}^{r_{2}} x^{3} d x=\dfrac{2 \alpha M}{r_{2}^{2}-r_{1}^{2}}\left[\dfrac{r_{2}^{4}-r_{1}^{4}}{4}\right] \\& \tau=\dfrac{M\left(r_{2}^{4}-r_{1}^{4}\right) \alpha}{2\left(r_{2}^{2}-r_{1}^{2}\right)}\end{aligned}$

question: The elastic limit of brass is $3.5 \times {10^{10}}\,N{\rm{/}}{m^2}$. Find the maximum load that can be applied to a brass wire of $0.75\,mm$ diameter without exceeding the elastic limit.
explanation:
Stress $ = \frac{F}{A}$; For elastic limit,${\rm{stress }} = 3.5 \times {10^{10}}\,N{\rm{/}}{m^2}{\rm{ (given)}}.$
${\rm{Thus}},{\rm{ }}F = \left( {\pi {r^2}} \right) \times {\rm{ stress}}$
$ = 3.14 \times {\left( {\frac{{0.75}}{2} \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)^2} \times 3.5 \times {10^{10}}$
$ = 3.14 \times \frac{{{{(0.75)}^2}}}{4} \times 3.5 \times {10^4}$

question: A stress of $6 \times {10^6}N{m^{ - 2}}$ required for breaking a material. The density $\rho$ of the material is $3 \times {10^3}\,kg\,{m^{ - 3}}.$ If the wire is to break under its own weight, the length of the wire made of that material should be (take, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
The minimum stress after which the wire
breaks, i.e.
${\rm{Stress}} = \frac{{{\rm{ Force }}}}{{{\rm{ Area }}}} = \frac{{mg}}{A}$
$ = \frac{{V\rho g}}{A} = \frac{{LA\rho g}}{A} = L\rho g$
Given, stress $ = 6 \times {10^6}N{m^{ - 2}},$
$\rho = 3 \times {10^3}kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$
and $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
$L = \frac{{{\rm{ Stress }}}}{{\rho g}} = \frac{{6 \times {{10}^6}}}{{3 \times {{10}^3} \times 10}}$
$ = 2 \times {10^2} = 200\,m$

question: Two blocks of masses $m$ and $M$ are connected by means of a metal wire of cross-sectional area shown in the figure. The system is then released. If $M = 2\,m,$ then the stress produced in the wire is :

tension in the wire, $T = \left( {\frac{{2mM}}{{m + M}}} \right)g$
${\text{Stress}} = \frac{{{\text{Tension}}}}{{{\text{Area}}}} = \frac{{2mM}}{{A\left( {m + M} \right)}}g$

question: A rope of  1$cm$ in diameter breaks if tension in it exceeds $500N$. The maximum tension that may be given to a similar rope of diameter 2 $cm$ is
explanation:
As the ropes are similar but have different diameters $(1\,cm\,{\text{and}}\,2\,cm)$, for maximum tension the stresses are also maximum and equal in both cases
$\therefore \sigma_{\max }=\dfrac{\left(F_{1}\right)_{\max }}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} d_{1}^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(F_{2}\right)_{\max }}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} d_{2}^{2}} \Rightarrow\left(F_{2}\right)_{\max }=\left(F_{1}\right)_{\max } \dfrac{d_{2}^{2}}{d_{1}^{2}} \text {. }$
Given $\left(F_{1}\right)_{\max }=500 \mathrm{~N}$,
$ \Rightarrow {\left( {{F_2}} \right)_{\max }} = 500 \times \frac{{{2^2}}}{{{1^2}}} = 2000\,N.{\text{ }}$

question: A lift of mass ' $M$ ' is tied with thick iron ropes. The maximum acceleration of the lift is ' $a$ '  $m/{s^2}$ and maximum safe stress is '$S'\,N/{m^2}$. The minimum diameter of the rope is
explanation:

is given by ${\sigma _{\max }} = \frac{{{\text{ maximum force }}}}{{{\text{ area }}}} = \frac{{M(g + a)}}{{\pi {{\left( {\frac{d}{2}} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{4M(g + a)}}{{\pi d}}$
(when the lift is accelerating upwards with acceleration a)
For maximum safe stress, ${\sigma _{\max {\text{ }}}} = S$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow S=\dfrac{4 M(g+a)}{\pi d^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow d=\sqrt{\dfrac{4 M(g+a)}{\pi S}}\end{aligned}$

question: One end of a uniform wire of length $L$ and weight  $w$ is attached rigidly to a point in roof and a weight ${w_1}$ is suspended from its lower end. If $S$ is the area of cross-section of the wire, the stress in the wire at a height of  $\frac{{3\,L}}{4}$ from its lower end is

$\therefore {\text{Stress}} = \frac{T}{S} = \left( {\frac{{{w_1} + \frac{{3w}}{4}}}{{\,S}}} \right)$

question: A steel cable with a radius of $1.5\,cm$ supports a chair lift at a ski area. If the maximum stress is not to exceed ${10^8}\,N/{m^2},$  what is the maximum load the cable can support?
explanation:

$(r) = 1.5\,cm = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,cm$

$ = 3.14 \times {\left( {1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)^2}\,{m^2}$
$ = 3.14 \times 2.25 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$

Maximum force $=$ Maximum stress $\times$
Area of cross-section
$=10^{8} \times\left(3.14 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4}\right) N=7.1 \times 10^{4} N$

question: A mild steel wire of length $2\,L$ and crosssectional area A is stretched well within elastic limit, horizontally between two pillars as shown in figure. A mass $m$ is suspended from the mid point of the wire. Strain in the wire is

question: Two blocks of masses $1\,kg$ and $2\,kg$ are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley as shown. The breaking stress of the metal is $\frac{{40}}{{3\pi }} \times {10^6}\,N/{m^2}$. If  $g = 10\,m/{s^2},$ then what should be minimum radius of the wire used if it is not to break?
explanation:
The tension in the string is

If $r$ is the minimum radius, then
Breaking stress $=\dfrac{40}{3 \pi} \times 10^{6}=\dfrac{40}{3 \pi r^{2}}$

question: A ring of radius $\mathrm{R}$ made of lead wire breaking strength $\sigma$ and density $\rho$, roatated about stationary vertical axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring. Calculate the number of rotations per second at which the ring ruptures
explanation:
Let us consider small part of ring, which subtend an angle $\theta$ at the centre. Due to rotation, each part of ring experience an outward force.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951581/preptracker/questions/q_35817_explanation_1764951580110.png
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951582/preptracker/questions/q_35817_explanation_1764951582432.png
Mass of the element $(d m)=\rho d V=\rho(A R \theta)$
$2 T \sin \theta / 2=(d m) \omega^{2} R$
$\Rightarrow T=\rho A \omega^{2} R^{2}$
The stress at any section of the ring
$\Rightarrow f=\dfrac{T}{A}=\dfrac{\rho A \omega^{2} R^{2}}{A}=\rho \omega^{2} R^{2}$
Rupture takes place when $f=\sigma$
$\Rightarrow \sigma=\rho \omega^{2} R^{2} \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho R^{2}}}$
and $\quad n=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega}{\rho R^{2}}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi R} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}}$
subject: Physics

question: Maximum excess pressure inside a thin-walled steel tube of radius $\mathrm{r}$ and thickness $\Delta r( <  < r)$, so that the tube would not rupture would be (breaking stress of steel is ${\sigma _{\max }}$ )

the net tension towards $C$ is balanced by
Pressure force on the surface
$2T\sin \theta = (\Delta P){\text{ Area }}$
$2T\Delta \theta = \Delta P({\text{lr}}2\Delta \theta )({\text{For}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{small}}{\mkern 1mu} \Delta \theta )$
$T = \Delta Pr\ell \quad (\Delta P = {\text{ excess Pressure }})$
The maximum stress developed across the cross section of the strip is
$\begin{aligned}& \sigma_{\max }=\dfrac{T}{\Delta A}=\dfrac{\Delta \operatorname{Pr} \ell}{\Delta r \ell} \\& \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P=\dfrac{\sigma_{\max } \Delta r}{r}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A steel wire of cross-sectional area $3 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,\,{m^2}$ can withstand a maximum strain of  ${10^{ - 3}}$. Young's modulus of steel is $2 \times {10^{11}}\,\,N/{m^2}.$ The maximum mass the wire can hold is (Take $g = 10\,\,m/{s^2}$ )
